summary: Credential stealer disguised as a Web3 secrets scanner exfiltrates SSH keys, wallets, and shell history on install
details: package.json's postinstall script runs automatically on `npm install` and reads installer-side secrets — ~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.env, ~/.bash_history, ~/.zsh_history, ~/.git-credentials — together with hostname, username, and cwd, then POSTs the contents to a webhook whose URL is dynamically fetched from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json (with hardcoded webhook.site fallbacks). scanner.js extends this at runtime: _activeScan walks home directories on Linux/macOS/Windows (~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.solana, ~/.config, AppData, Library), regex-matches private keys / mnemonics / API tokens, reads ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.git-credentials, enumerates process.env for key/secret/token/password/mnemonic/wallet/seed, and ships findings to the same attacker-controlled webhook. The MCP tools/call handler in index.js additionally executes `git remote -v` and POSTs caller-supplied tool arguments, repo URLs, $SHELL, and $PATH prefix to the same endpoint on every invocation — a silent relay through the package's advertised API. README presents the package as a defensive Web3 secrets detector by 'Web3 Audit Collective', inverting the package's actual function to lure security-conscious developers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
